data.title: The Davenport Funds Q4 2025 Stock Market Update
data.description:
Davenport Asset Management is a boutique money manager founded in 1984 with over $13 billion in assets under management. Our competitive advantages come from our history, partnership, independence, experience, and process refined over the 160-year history of our firm. We are long-term investors. We believe a consistent investment discipline combined with risk management leads to out-performance over a complete market cycle with lower volatility.

Well, That Was Interesting... What Now?

This past year brought a dizzying series of events. We had DOGE, tariffs, the Middle East, the “Big Beautiful Bill”, the Fed and all things artificial intelligence (AI) give way to a dramatic swoon and even more dramatic recovery (oh and don’t forget a government shutdown that markets largely ignored). In fact, it’s truly hard to believe we fit everything into one year! Ultimately, all the action culminated in another year of surprisingly strong returns for the broader equity indices. The S&P 500® Index and Russell 2000® Index finished 2025 up 17.88% and 12.81%, respectively, while the NASDAQ® Composite finished up 21.14%.

To review, the market started the year on a rough note with first quarter declines of 4.27% and 9.48%, respectively, for the S&P and Russell. Policy uncertainty, specifically DOGE and tariffs, weighed on investor sentiment. Declines went into overdrive in early April after “Liberation Day” and the official proclamation of steep tariffs on trading partners. At one point, the market declined 12.60% in just four days and was down 18.33% from its high. We then staged a stunning rally as tariffs were softened and DOGE faded into the background. Subsequently, gains were furthered as the economy remained strong, Congress passed Trump’s BBB and the Federal Reserve resumed interest rate cuts. All the while, AI was in the background fueling massive capital expenditures and stoking investor excitement.

Speaking of AI, technology and AI-related stocks led the charge again in 2025. The tech and communications services sectors advanced 23.83% and 32.47%, respectively, trouncing the rest of the market. AI darling Nvidia (NVDA), which now represents 7.74% of the S&P, was up 38.87% after a 171.17% gain the prior year. A steady stream of huge spending announcements from tech leaders helped induce the gains. Ultimately, a gold rush mindset seemed to develop across the AI ecosystem and we saw the fervor spread to more speculative corners of the market. So called MEME stocks, which gain popularity on social media, and unprofitable AI/tech stocks posted explosive moves higher. As seen in other investment crazes, people started betting on “stories” with little regard for the price they were paying. Case in point, 18 of the top 20 performers in the Russell 3000® Index from April 1st through the end of November were unprofitable companies!

We don’t doubt the promise of AI. Advancement and deployment of the technology may accelerate from here and bring new applications that are currently unfathomable. However, there are many risks and unknowns for investors. What could go wrong? For one, investors may begin to question how the leading players will earn adequate returns on massive capital outlays for computing power. Gartner projects global AI spending will reach $2 trillion in 2026. According to the Guardian, OpenAI is expected to spend $1.4 trillion over eight years whereas the company’s current revenue expectation for the end of 2025 is $20 billion. Much of the spending appears to be driven by FOMO (fear of missing out) and the path to returns is very unclear. Investor Howard Marks recently likened the spending boom to “building an airplane while it’s in flight.” We also note the landscape is rapidly shifting and many companies, including current leaders, could be upended by new business models as the technology advances. Whether it’s search engines, software or semiconductor companies, many of today’s winners could quickly become tomorrow’s losers (and vice versa). Hence, it seems foolhardy to think one can predict outcomes with a high degree of certainty.

We aren’t labeling this a bubble or calling for a top in AI- related stocks. While underweight technology in general, we own some of the large cap tech leaders and remain bullish on them. However, it does seem reasonable for this area, especially more speculative corners, to cool off after an extended run. While AI may be in the 1st or 2nd inning of development, valuations for many of the high-flyers appear to be in later innings and investor crowding seems extreme. We could either see a market rotation or broadening of returns. In other words, we could see investors gravitate to other areas of the market that have struggled. Many stocks, particularly in more economically sensitive areas, are beaten up despite the indices being near all-time highs. While the S&P 500, which has essentially become a large cap technology index, looks expensive at 22.14x forward earnings estimate, the equal-weighted S&P is much more reasonable at 16.74x. The stimulative effects of Trump’s fiscal policy could fuel improvement in more cyclical industries that have lagged. The same could be said for ongoing support from the Federal Reserve. Also helping, in 2026 we won’t have the noise of tariffs or DOGE, which seemed to cast a temporary pall over both consumer and corporate sentiment this past year.

We have been focusing much of our efforts on those stocks that have been cast aside as investors have focused elsewhere. It hasn’t paid recently to have differentiated perspectives or be valuation sensitive. Jumping on momentum bandwagons has proven much more fruitful. However, we believe the market’s sun could shine elsewhere some point soon and can’t stomach the risk associated with many of today’s highflyers. Our conservative approach has weighed on relative performance of late, but we’ve seen this type of market dynamic before (most notably in the late 1990s) and it ultimately paid to look where other investors weren’t looking. Thank you for your trust and all the best in 2026!

Sincerely,

George L. Smith III, CFA®

Chairman, Investment Policy Committee

data.title: Emerging Markets: The Next Phase In Market-Broadening
data.description:
By Shannon L. Saccocia, CFA

At the same time developed markets have been generating policy and macro noise, emerging markets have been quietly outperforming. We expect EM risk assets to continue to deliver.

Even if the Federal Reserve pauses its rate cuts next week, as widely expected, this is unlikely to slow the emerging markets rally we have seen across equities and bonds over the past year or more.

The inextricable relationship between the direction of U.S. monetary policy and emerging market risk assets remains powerful, but this time the rally is not just about the Fed. Over the past 18 months, many emerging economies have moved earlier and more decisively than the U.S. in tackling inflation, leaving real policy rates positive, external balances healthier and fiscal trajectories more stable than in several large developed markets.

At the same time, earnings revisions have turned more favorable for a number of key emerging market sectors, particularly in Asia, where companies are central to the buildout of AI-related hardware and digital infrastructure. This is therefore a story of strengthening economic fundamentals, a widening growth differential and a more supportive domestic policy backdrop, turning emerging markets, in our view, from a tactical allocation into a more central component of the next phase of market-broadening.

Overweight on EM

For many of these reasons, we were already constructive on emerging markets. As of this quarter, we are more bullish—our Asset Allocation Committee has upgraded its view on emerging market equities to overweight, complementing longer-standing overweights on emerging market bonds and currencies.

Importantly, this upgrade largely reflects India being raised to overweight from underweight—underpinned by lower valuations, improving macro momentum and potential for foreign inflows—and Brazil’s elevation to overweight from at target, supported by monetary easing and commodities demand, a factor also benefiting other resource rich emerging economies. This brings these two markets in line with the overweight we have had on China since last year.

Crucially, our constructive stance on emerging markets is not confined to these three markets, nor is it purely a valuation or policy call. We see a broader opportunity set developing across emerging economies, particularly in Asia, where reforms, improving corporate governance and rising participation in the AI and digital infrastructure value chain are reinforcing the macro story and benefiting key markets.

Korea’s equity market has been a clear example. The KOSPI index hit fresh record highs last week, supported by foreign buying concentrated in AI semiconductor companies. Leadership extends beyond a few index heavyweights to high bandwidth memory suppliers and semiconductor equipment makers, as investors focus on the full AI supply chain. Our research points to Korea as one of the next major hubs for AI-related investment, supported by trends in patent filings and capital spending.

Cross-Asset Diversification

This highlights how emerging markets can provide a compelling complement to, and source of diversification from, U.S. mega-cap technology stocks which, despite delivering exceptional returns, leave investors heavily exposed to a narrow set of business models, regulatory regimes and valuation assumptions.

Emerging markets, by contrast, offer a different mix, including a greater representation of cyclical sectors such as financials, materials and industrials, which are linked to domestic development and global trade rather than only to digital platforms. Many emerging economies also provide exposure to structural themes like demographics and urbanization, which are largely absent in developed markets.

The case for broadening into emerging markets is not just an equity story, either. Emerging market sovereign and corporate debt have evolved from a niche, high beta segment into a deep, differentiated opportunity set.

Recent performance across emerging markets rates and credit has been robust, in many instances outperforming developed market equivalents, reflecting a combination of higher starting yields, earlier policy adjustment and improving fundamentals. This has been reinforced by a more supportive external backdrop—including a softer U.S. dollar and stabilizing global growth—as well as, in many cases, more contained debt burdens and default rates than in developed markets, enabling the capture of higher yields without a proportional deterioration in credit quality.

Moving to a Core Allocation

In our view, emerging markets now offer: a macro growth premium at a time when developed market growth is only slowly reaccelerating from a lower base; a way to broaden equity exposure beyond U.S. mega cap concentration into cyclical, domestic demand and AI infrastructure themes; and access to a more mature, diversified emerging market debt universe where fundamentals have, in many cases, improved relative to developed peers. Emerging markets also stand as a potential beneficiary of Fed easing and a softer dollar rather than a casualty of tightening.

The challenge, then, is not the lack of opportunity, but the need for selectivity: distinguishing between countries and companies that are genuinely reforming, innovating and strengthening governance and those that are not. For investors willing to look beyond familiar U.S. names, emerging markets increasingly look less like a tactical allocation and more like a central pillar of a broader, more resilient global allocation.

What to Watch For

  • Monday 1/19:
    • U.S. Holiday
    • Eurozone Consumer Price Index
  • Wednesday 1/21:
    • U.K. Consumer Price Index
  • Thursday 1/22:
    • U.S. GDP
    • U.S. Initial Jobless Claims
    • U.S. Core Personal Consumption Expenditure (PCE) Price Index
    • Japan BoJ Interest Rate Decision
  • Friday 1/23:
    • U.S. Manufacturing Purchasing Managers’ Index
    • U.S. Services Purchasing Managers’ Index

data.title: Why I'm Selling Sellas Life Sciences Ahead Of The REGAL Reveal
data.description:
Sellas Life Sciences’ stock (SLS) has risen >300% in the past year ahead of Phase 3 data for its peptide immunotherapy, galinpepimut-S (GPS), in patients with AML in second remission.

The Phase 3 REGAL trial looks at GPS versus the “best available treatment” (BAT). As of December 26th (72 events), the trial is nearing the 80 events that will trigger the final analysis.

Because I have not yet come across this stock, I wanted to take a closer look at it before the REGAL data drops to see if it is worth chasing or not.

The REGAL Reality: Historical Failures and Statistical Overreach

Most Phase 3 oncology OS trials are powered for HR targets around 0.75 to 0.80, which translates into a 20 to 25% relative reduction in hazard. REGAL’s target HR (0.636) is partly a bug of just 80 events. In any event, it sets a much higher bar for GPS to jump over.

REGAL has other features that can add noise. It is an open-label trial that compares GPS to BAT, which can include observation, hypomethylating agents, venetoclax, and other treatments. While consistent with AML treatment guidelines, which are highly individualized, it makes REGAL a lot more heterogeneous.

One of the first things I do when I research a different modality is I ask myself if it has been evaluated before. Here, Sellas is not the first to explore WT1 as a target in AML. A randomized placebo-controlled Phase 2 trial of the WT1 peptide vaccine OCV-501 in elderly AML patients in first remission reported no significant difference in disease-free survival or OS against placebo. This was despite an immunogenic response in a large minority of patients.

OCV-501 is direct evidence that WT1 peptide vaccination in AML remission can fail clinically even when it is immunogenic. AML relapse biology is tricky and features immune escape mechanisms and T-cell dysfunction. So antigen presence alone may not result in effective, durable antileukemic T-cell activity.

REGAL somewhat learned from OCV-501. But in the classic biomarker enrichment sense, it did not. REGAL is not enriched for a WT1-high subset at baseline, even though WT1 is broadly expressed in AML. Now, REGAL is set up to discover correlates of benefit, but those same measures are not used to enrich enrollment upfront (outside of lymphocyte parameters).

We do have small, early data in a similar AML population for GPS. But the most cited survival comparisons come from tiny groups and often use historical or post-hoc match controls. So we cannot extrapolate too much from this.

Management frames REGAL taking longer than expected as evidence that GPS “actually works.” But the reality is that this can also be consistent with both arms living longer. This would actually make the most sense. REGAL is open-label and compares GPS to BAT. So it is totally possible that "heterogeneous controls plus evolving real-world AML practice" can lift control arms' survival above the study's planning assumptions. The higher the median OS of the pooled population becomes, the more difficult it will be for GPS to hit a low HR.

If you break it down, there are many different scenarios that could result in a delayed “80th event” timeline. A GPS win would look something like “18.9 months versus 12 months.” Alternatively, an HR near 0.8 may look like “17.5 months versus 14 months.” And an HR near 1.0 would appear as “16 months versus 16 months.”

Bear in mind that while management is privy to overall events, they are not aware of “what events are taking place in what arm.” So they are blinded to what matters, and any assumptions regarding GPS efficacy are just that, assumptions.

Pipeline

Beyond GPS, Sellas is also evaluating SLS009, a selective CDK9 inhibitor. The company plans to soon enroll newly diagnosed first-line AML patients (see pipeline for other indications).

Although this asset may prove to be a "dark horse" in the future, I wouldn't expect it to shield investors from REGAL-related volatility. And naturally, testing an asset in earlier lines is always met with more competition and a higher bar for efficacy and safety. Lastly, CDK9 inhibition has been explored here in the past (with limited OS benefits or toxicity issues), although SLS009 is one of the few newer and more selective CDK9 inhibitors in development.

Final Words

Past findings with WT1 targeting in AML do not inspire confidence. The bar is high in REGAL. And, contrary to popular opinion, "longer" might not be better.

Prospects elsewhere are dicey. First-line AML is very competitive. Sellas does not have a lot of cash (~$71.8 million as of December 31 plus $26.5 million from a January exercise of common stock warrants) and “burns” roughly $8 million/quarter.

So its stock, which has been subject to a great deal of dilution recently (as of January 7, shares outstanding were ~170 million), is quite dependent on REGAL outcomes, which I think are very likely to disappoint. If this happens, the stock could drop near cash value (<$1/share).

So I'm going to recommend a “Sell” rating. If one is holding SLS in a barbell portfolio, it may be wise to reduce or limit one's position ahead of this major catalyst.

Risks to the thesis include a compelling win in REGAL that would change the AML treatment landscape, positive pipeline developments, and M&A developments.

data.title: Joby Aviation: A Billion Dollars Says They Can Fly (But Can They Earn?)
data.description:
Introduction

Joby Aviation (JOBY) is the one that got away for me, even though I rated this stock as a buy at the beginning of June, before the initial price surge, and it was my first look at this stock.

Later, at the end of September, I wrote another article about upcoming catalysts in 2026 but downgraded the stock due to the elevated valuation. Since then, the stock has declined by about 7%, and I am very pleased with my ratings, despite not following my own advice and not benefiting from the rally myself. The reason for this I explained in the previous piece, which, by the way, is surprisingly relevant today, and most of the developments I talked about there are something to take into consideration also today.

We are in the middle of the quarter, between earnings, but it’s a good time to take another look at Joby because the earnings don’t really add any new information. This is a company that is still mostly pre-revenue, although it started operating via its newest acquisition. The volume, AKA an interest of investors toward this stock, is lower than it has been throughout 2025, but I assume it won’t stay in this state for long, as catalysts keep coming, and Joby’s management knows how to present the news to make the headlines (one of the most important skills nowadays for the stock momentum).

Peeking forward, I rate Joby as a Hold once again for the elevated valuation level, regulatory concerns, and financial position that worries me this time around more than the last one. Even though I am not brave enough to jump in at the current level, I find this company fascinating, and I think some of the developments will stick with us for a very long time and change how we see transportation and logistics. At the same time, I am very skeptical about the passenger-oriented side of the business.

Q3 2025

After going through the results, I have three strong insights that shaped the way I saw Joby this time around:

  1. The company moves strategically with its plan and checks the boxes of the investors and analysts methodically. In my previous article, I talked about every catalyst that awaits Joby going forward, and in the earnings call, it looked like the management took my checklist and decided to address every point. I really appreciate the management that knows how to talk to the investors. It’s a very important skill, and sometimes, it can be enough to create a high valuation for the company solely on this communication—greeting to Tesla (TSLA) management; they are the best there.

  2. The management seems to be worried about ramping up the manufacturing, and it looks like they project this could be the most serious bottleneck in 2026 for Joby.

The bottleneck is going to be how fast we can ramp manufacturing.

However, I can’t help but notice elevated spending levels. I agree that a supply shortage at the time of strong demand isn’t the most ideal situation, but at the moment, Joby is not yet operational. It needs to get the final approval from the FAA (Federal Aviation Administration). Joby is the most advanced company in the process of obtaining Type Certification, but this doesn’t mean they will be flying tomorrow, and this company doesn’t have a real backlog yet. They have several pretty solid contracts, but they do not involve the payment just yet. When you read the terms of those, you would see terms like “it has signed a letter of intent” or “potential delivery of up to 200 Joby aircraft.” I can’t stress enough how much I wish that those contracts would materialize into real revenue, but having a few attempts on businesses myself, I learned a tough lesson: everyone will want your product until they need to actually pay for it, and you earn the money only after the other side is not available to ask for that money back.

So instead of looking at the promises and attempts, I was noticing the significant outflows. I want to add a couple of quotes from earnings so you can see it for yourself:

We remain on track to hit the upper end of our full year 2025 guidance of $500 million to $540 million in use of cash, cash equivalents, in short-term investments, and that includes the impact of our Blade acquisition.

Our Q3 use of cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments totaled $147 million, $35 million higher than last quarter. That was primarily due to an extra payroll run in Q3 versus Q2, growth in operating expenses, working capital changes, and one-time costs related to our Blade acquisition, which accounted for $6 million. This spending also included about $30 million on property and equipment, up $1 million from last quarter.

The additional spending is significant across the board—more on salaries, operational expenses, property, and equipment, meaning more money to every type of expense from the income statement. I am okay with companies that increase their spending as a result of elevated demand. But demand should be evident by the inflows of capital. Even prepayment of 1% is an obligation that looks much more serious than an “intent,” and it could serve as justification for the expenses.

Just last week, Joby announced the acquisition of an additional manufacturing facility, which supports my suspicions that we will see only growing spending moving forward. It is also supported by the guidance.

So, for now, we have the management that communicates perfectly with shareholders, but the company that isn’t very careful with the money.

Guidance for cash use was reiterated at the upper end of the previously stated range, now explicitly including Blade’s impact.

  1. Management says that they have a sufficient amount of cash, but in fact, this only shows how comfortable Joby is with diluting its shareholders.

At the end of the quarter, we had approximately $978 million in cash and short-term investments. In October, we added net proceeds of approximately $576 million further strengthening our position. This gives us the financial strength to continue to lead the industry and bring new innovations to markets across the globe.

So, addressing your (or more likely my) worry, the company has enough cash to cover the burn. At the same time, as a shareholder, I wouldn’t be happy with this current situation, as this money is coming from your value. Every quarter, there is a dilution, and it is really significant. According to my calculation (911-765)/765, the dilution rate is 19%, meaning you can subtract it from the gains you’ve seen during this timeframe.

It was still worth it, of course, but don’t forget that the stock was way cheaper. If the same dilution rate stays through 2026, you would need the stock to be up by at least that to stay breakeven.

Developments

Not everything is doom and gloom, of course. This company has a real advancement on the competitors and truly interesting developments and contracts.

First of all, the company started seeing positive revenues from its acquisition of Blade. Blade is a company that operates in passenger transportation, mostly with helicopters, and while Joby’s aircraft isn’t used for these operations yet, they’ll start to be as soon as Joby gets permission.

Another development, the one I am most excited about, is the one with L3Harris (LHX). Quoting a piece of news from Seeking Alpha:

L3Harris (LHX) plans to equip Joby’s (JOBY) commercial hybrid aircraft to address defense applications, such as contested logistics, “loyal wingman” operations, and low-altitude support. The collaboration draws on the U.S. government’s $9 billion FY26 budget allocation for the development of resilient, autonomous, and hybrid aircraft.

In my previous articles, I said I was skeptical about passenger transportation because it’s not really comfortable, and it won’t work as a taxi; you would still have ports or stations of departure/arrival. It most likely won’t be as cheap as a regular taxi, even though this is an ultimate goal. Looking at the expense levels of these companies, I am very doubtful they can seriously be cheap and earn money at the same time. Another argument that I have is that we can’t even meaningfully adopt robotaxis until now. Do you really think we will have operational autonomous aircraft flying over our heads any time soon? Also, I believe the closer we get to having those, the harder the regulation will get. Just recently, a piece of news about permission hardening affected stocks in this industry.

At the same time, I really like the possible use cases of this one for military/hospitals/firefighters and other governmental institutions. I really think it can be a future tech that will be quickly adopted by the developed countries, and I like seeing that Joby is already working in this direction. For now, it’s only a defense, but as soon as they are done there, new ways of use could appear.

Of course, there are many more developments, of which I talked about in my latest piece. It includes the Dubai and Saudis.

Valuation & Risks

The valuation is one of the biggest risks for Joby as of today. This company is very expensive, and in general, due to the lack of sales and earnings, of course, it’s hard to evaluate it. Looking at the Price-to-Book ratio, which, in my opinion, is a questionable way of evaluating companies, this stock is anywhere between 135% to 330% overvalued compared to the sector. Translating to the language of retail investors, the stock is so expensive that any piece of uncertain news (not only for Joby, but in general in the U.S./the world) could send it plunging just because people are afraid of sticking to names like that when the market turns bearish.

Other risks include high spending, a lack of material proof for contracts, and regulatory changes that can happen at any time.

Conclusion

Before writing this conclusion, I’ve read everything that’s written before, and I have to say, this piece turned out way more critical than I intended. Maybe it’s winter mood; maybe it’s because I am extra worried when companies that don’t earn are so comfortable with investing and spending. I still think Joby is the most advanced player among the competitors. I really like the direction. I recognize all of the catalysts in 2026, and I like the communication from the management. At the same time, I see so many ways things can go wrong, and the stock can plunge. At the moment, I would be uncomfortable opening a position in this stock, although I would continue holding it if I were in from my initial buy rating.

data.title: CoreWeave: Why I'm Buying This Controversial AI Stock
data.description:
CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) is a name that really started to receive a lot of attention in 2025. The debate on whether to buy or sell the stock has been quite fierce, and students of the market will probably agree that the popularity of these "hype" stocks usually doesn’t carry over into a second year. Of course, the operative word here is "usually" and there are many reasons to believe that CoreWeave might actually have the ability to break this trend and build on the gains in share prices that have accumulated during the last twelve months. Large pillars of my bullish argument rest on the fact that the company’s order backlog has nearly doubled on a quarterly basis (moving close to the $56 billion mark), which gives the market another example of a growing AI infrastructure player that is being limited more by supply than by demand. Recent pullbacks have sent the stock well below $100, and I will use these cheaper entry points to establish new long positions and initiate my outlook with a "buy" rating as we begin trading in 2026.

For the third-quarter period, EPS was still negative (with a loss of $0.22 per share), but CoreWeave did beat consensus estimates on both the top line and bottom line. Revenue was the real highlight (at $1.36 billion) because this figure indicated monstrous YoY gains of 134%. Net losses came in at $110 million, but this was a significant improvement from the $360 million loss that was recorded in Q3 2024. Over the last month, CRWV shares have mostly traded under pressure, even falling below the $70 mark at one point. Despite the positives seen in the Q3 release, I think we can largely attribute this weakness to CoreWeave’s subdued or mediocre guidance for the full-year period. 2025 revenues are now expected to post slightly above $5 billion, and I think this is focusing the market’s attention on Q4 disappointments that might not have been fully priced in previously.

Maybe it’s not surprising that many (more bearish) analysts have largely dismissed CoreWeave as a "trendy" stock that is unlikely to maintain its multiple because share prices have rallied by more than 125% since last March. But it’s very difficult to ignore revenue growth rates like these. CoreWeave is also showing a backlog of nearly $56 billion (contracted power of 2.9 gigawatts), and major contracts were announced during the third-quarter period. Specifically, a new six-year contract with Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) valued at $14.2 billion has been detailed in addition to a $6.5 billion increase for an existing contract with OpenAI. Clearly, CoreWeave is already solidifying a strong position in the industry and developing important relationships with the leading hyperscalers.

Now, I do not want to appear as though I am simply waxing poetic about the latest fad stock. There are obvious issues and risks that go beyond the extremely overextended valuation metrics (which I will address in a moment). But I think CoreWeave could also experience some unforeseen headwinds because of certain supply constraints. In many instances in the AI buildout, we are seeing companies complain about a widening (and seemingly insatiable) need for power. However, CoreWeave has already shown vulnerabilities connected to semi-finished powered shell data centers experiencing unexpected delays. Of course, these are not the types of events that instill confidence, so this will be one factor that long investors will need to pay special attention to in 2026.

Since this stock has been such a high-flyer, we must take an assessment of the potential risks that are facing potential CRWV investors right now. One of the areas that concerns me most is CoreWeave’s debt-to-equity ratio. With equity of $3.88 billion and total debt of $18.81 billion, we have $16.87 billion and an extremely elevated TTM D/E ratio of about 4.85x. This can be explained as the use of aggressive leverage in order to achieve sizable expansion. But when we add to this the fact that FY2025 expense guidance came in at $1.21-1.25 billion, we have to understand that default risks are real if rates increase or if we start to see a real showdown in AI demand spending. At the very least, this puts a lot of pressure on the balance sheet, and this alone might be enough to deter some conservative investors.

Free cash flow over the trailing twelve-month period is also deeply negative (worse than -$8 billion). In order to fund capex, CoreWeave is burning cash at rates that are clearly extreme (some analysts might even say at rates that are unsustainable). In the chart above, we can see that CoreWeave is an outlier when viewing the peer group in terms of the free cash flow yield metric. Massive deficits in free cash flow require external financing constantly (either through equity dilution or debt). The main risk here for CRWV long positions would be seen if credit spreads expand and equity value is reduced.

Fortunately, these negative concerns are balanced out by CoreWeave’s revenue growth expectations, which are also "off the charts" (but in a good way). With more than $4.3 billion in revenue (TTM), this is activity that can only be described as "hyper growth" that has been fueled by large hyperscaler contracts. Now, we can’t guarantee that these trends will continue, but they certainly send signals that suggest that top-line growth could continue to expand on strong demand and justify premium valuations. Based on what I am seeing, one of the most common criticisms of the CoreWeave long thesis revolves around the stock’s high EV/EBITDA metrics (which stand above 28x over the trailing twelve-month period). However, that ratio shrinks sizably when looking at the forward numbers, and I don’t think it’s enough to simply view TTM EV/EBITDA in isolation. Conversely, I think it could be argued that CoreWeave’s multiple starts to look relatively inexpensive when annual revenue growth expectations are added to the equation.

CoreWeave’s margin profile is also quite impressive. Granted, there are other stocks in the peer group that boast higher gross margin figures. Snowflake Inc. (SNOW), Okta, Inc. (OKTA), Datadog, Inc. (DDOG), and MongoDB, Inc. (MDB) are all showing gross margin metrics that are stronger right now. But none of these other stocks are looking spectacular when viewing performances from the perspective of EBITDA margin, and I think it can be said that CoreWeave has the most balanced margin profile in this entire group. For investors looking at earlier-stage stocks in a business that is capital intensive, this is helpful because CoreWeave is demonstrating cost management practices that are efficient (and improving relative to prior years). As revenue continues to scale, strong profitability figures and long-term shareholder value start to become more attainable. Operational leverage is being reflected in CoreWeave’s positive EBITDA expansion (reversing from the negative margins we were seeing in prior years), and operating income guidance looks strong for the full-year period.

On the daily charts, we are actually looking as though we could be on the verge of a breakout because share prices have already broken through the 20-Day and 50-Day exponential moving averages (EMAs). Of course, the main "line in the sand here" is going to be the 100-Day, and that reading is still providing some resistance at the moment. However, if we look at the momentum reading on the same timeframe, we can see that the moving average convergence divergence is already crossing above the zero line, and we are still quite a distance away from reaching overbought territory. As a result, I think there is enough momentum to convincingly break above the 100-Day EMA because most of the recent spikes in trading volume have been bullish (going back to roughly the middle of December 2025).

In the other direction, I will start to believe that my bullish assessments are wrong if share prices fall below important historical support zones near $70.43 (which marks the low from December 19th, 2025). If this occurs, I would start to expect a deeper retracement in the downside direction and begin to look for a test of the $63.80 lows that printed earlier during the same month. On balance, I am seeing a reverse Head and Shoulders pattern developing on this timeframe, and this is a bullish reversal pattern that suggests a long term might have been recorded into the final weeks of 2025. This chart view largely aligns with my positive fundamental stance on the stock, and this confluence of signals implies that we might actually be seeing a constructive pullback in share prices that can be used to either establish new positions in CRWV or add to existing long trades in the stock. With a high-flying name like this, it is always important to keep position sizes somewhat smaller because unexpected volatility can quickly erode gains for investors that are not positioned properly. With all of this in mind, I have initiated coverage with a "buy" rating and started a CRWV long trade near current levels in order to confirm my positive stance.

data.title: Agree Realty Vs. Essential Properties Realty Trust: What Is The Best REIT For 2026?
data.description:
Two of the most popular net lease REITs are:

  • Agree Realty Corporation (ADC)
  • Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT)

They are so popular because they have historically managed to grow a lot faster than their larger peer, Realty Income (O), and that faster growth has resulted in greater total returns.

Agree Realty Corporation vs. Realty Income:

Essential Properties Realty Trust vs. Realty Income:

But Which One is the Best REIT to Buy Today?

Many investors seem to be favouring EPRT for one particular reason: it has been able to grow even faster than ADC. The gap in growth was remarkably big in 2025 as EPRT was able to source a large volume of new acquisitions, earning them significant spreads over their cost of capital.

Even then, if I had to buy one, I would favor ADC. It may not grow quite as fast during good years, but I expect its long-term growth to be a lot safer, steadier, and more predictable than that of EPRT.

Before becoming a writer here on Seeking Alpha, I worked at a private equity firm that focused primarily on net lease property investments. Now, I am not proclaiming myself to be the world's leading expert in net lease investing, but I certainly know that industry and investment model better than any other, and I would wager to say I understand it better than most REIT investors.

Fundamentally, net lease investing is about real estate. That probably sounds obvious, but lots of investors treat net lease properties basically like asset-backed corporate bonds. They don't think much about the location or tenant quality, just about the attractiveness of the contracts.

That may work fine over the short-to-mid term for a REIT as it targets properties at higher cap rates, earning them larger immediate spreads over their cost of capital. It leads to greater near-term accretion and faster FFO per share growth.

EPRT has clearly mastered that.

However, those higher cap rates will often come at a steep long-term cost, as net leases can't cover up flaws in location, building quality, fungibility, or tenant strength forever.

The management at ADC understands this and treats net lease investing like any other form of real estate investment. High-quality buildings in high-quality locations occupied by high-quality tenants retain their value over long periods of time and provide consistent cash flows.

This is the big fundamental difference between ADC and EPRT.

ADC is focusing on steady long-term value creation by acquiring the best assets with superior long-term prospects, typically at lower immediate cap rates and spreads, whereas EPRT is seeking to maximise immediate accretion by acquiring properties often at higher cap rates but with worse long-term prospects.

This is evident if you look at this table:

ADC EPRT
Investment-grade tenants 67% 0%
Market exposure Primary Secondary
Biggest category Grocery stores (safer) Car washes (riskier)

Now, this does not mean that EPRT's approach is bad. It has generated very strong returns so far, and it may continue to outperform far into the future.

But it also comes with greater long-term risks. Its tenants are primarily smaller middle-market sub-investment-grade companies. They have riskier balance sheets, worse access to capital, and shorter track records in most cases. Importantly, the properties they operate may be profitable today, but since they are typically located in secondary markets with lower barriers to entry, they are highly exposed to future competition, which could worsen their profitability over time, increasing the risk of future costly vacancies and/or rent cuts.

Let's Look at an Example:

A REIT like EPRT may buy a net-leased taco shop located in a small town at an 8.5% cap rate with a 15-year lease and strong rent coverage, resulting in a large 200 basis point spread over its cost of capital. That may sound great on paper, but a lot can change over a 15-year period. Often, there is little to stop a competitor from opening another taco shop just across the street, potentially having a major impact on the property's profitability. Smaller markets often have ample available land to build new properties, and the smaller non-rated tenant is at much greater risk of then defaulting on its lease.

On the other hand, a REIT like ADC may instead target a Chick-fil-A net lease property in a better-developed market at a 6.5% cap rate, resulting in just a 100 basis point spread. The immediate accretion is lower, but given that the property is well-located, enjoys greater barriers to entry, and has a stronger tenant, it is much more likely to remain profitable and grow in value over the long run. This will ultimately likely lead to stronger total returns with lower risk over a multi-decade period.

Both investment strategies can work, but investors should not ignore the vast difference in asset quality and long-term risk.

ADC is the higher-quality net lease REIT with safer long-term prospects, and that warrants a premium valuation, even despite potentially growing more slowly in the immediate term.

The market has correctly priced ADC at a slight premium, but I would argue that the difference should be even bigger. Based on P/NAV, EPRT is actually more expensive than ADC:

ADC EPRT
P/FFO 16.7x 15.5x
Dividend Yield 4.4% 4%
P/NAV 1.1% 1.16x

For this reason, I view ADC as a better investment opportunity right now and hold a position in it as part of our Retirement Portfolio at High Yield Landlord.

data.title: Sana Biotechnology Stock: Promising T1D Science, But Wait For Dips
data.description:
Sana Biotechnology, Inc. (SANA) is a biotech company that develops an ex vivo hypoimmune therapy and an in vivo fusogen-based delivery system. This way, they can help treat cancer and autoimmune diseases. Their ex vivo platform is designed to help transplanted cells evade immune rejection, and the in vivo fusogen system is intended to selectively deliver genetic therapeutic material. SANA's priority is to advance the studies for UP421, as early human validation of the immune evasion of transplanted islet cells that produce insulin. Also, SC451 has become their stem cell-derived scalable product, with an IND planned for 2026. However, I think that SANA’s current valuation is a bit too rich at these levels, which is why I recommend waiting for dips before adding to your position.

In Vivo And Ex Vivo Systems

Sana Biotechnology, Inc. is a biotech working on therapies for type 1 diabetes (T1D), blood cancers, and autoimmune conditions. SANA was founded back in 2018 and is currently headquartered in Seattle, Washington, with operations in Cambridge, South San Francisco, and Rochester. I previously covered SANA with a “Buy” in March last year, and since then, the stock has appreciated by approximately 91.6%, which is why I thought it was worthwhile revisiting this name.

As a quick recap, SANA's platforms have two main programs. The first one focuses on hypoimmune (HIP) technology. It aims to make transplanted cells less likely to be rejected by the immune system. The second program uses in vivo cell engineering based on fusogen technology to deliver a therapeutic payload to specific cells. Fusogen is a biological process that enables the merger of two membranes, which can be used on a virus-like particle (VLP) to selectively fuse with a chosen cell type to drop genetic material.

Additionally, SANA’s HIP pipeline includes two candidates to treat type 1 diabetes (T1D): UP421 and SC451. First, UP421 is studied as proof-of-concept with cadaveric transplantation evading immune rejection, while SC451 is intended to apply the same concept in a more repeatable way using a master cell bank as starting material for stem cell-derived islets. However, note that both of these candidates are evidently pre-clinical concepts, so they should be several years away from any regulatory approvals.

Two Main Platforms

Nevertheless, it’s worth mentioning that UP421 is tested in a first-in-human safety study, which is investigator-sponsored. The hypothesis is that genetically modified Langerhans islet cells from a deceased organ donor can be transplanted and help to regain insulin production in T1D patients. SANA uses cadaveric islet cells that were gene-edited to evade immunity. And early proof-of-concept results may become visible quickly using C-peptide as a marker to show that the transplanted islet cells are producing insulin, because injected insulin does not generate it. So, I do think this approach has some promise, and if they announce favorable early data, I would imagine they’d move quickly into a more formal Phase 1 trial.

Furthermore, SC451 (preclinical stages as well) does have a planned IND application in 2026. This candidate will use the same immune evasion characteristics from UP421, but apply them in a more scalable way. Here, they won’t use cadaveric islets. Instead, SANA will replace them with stem cells. The idea is to use a single universal donor stem cell, edit it to avoid rejection, create a master cell bank, and differentiate it into islets for transplantation in many patients.

Aside from these two assets, SANA’s second platform produces in vivo CAR T using a fusogen system. Their approach engineers a VLP to target T cells to deliver a genetic payload. The genetic material contains a chimeric antigen receptor (CAR), which is modified to recognize a target marker on a cell. Then it activates the T cell when it binds to that target. This differs from traditional methods because traditional delivery systems would bring the CAR-encoding genetic material directly to T cells without requiring previous processes. Thus, in vivo CAR T cells could be off-the-shelf products and improve access, while reducing cost and time for producers. SANA’s candidates with this platform are SG299 (prior version) and SG293 (current candidate), and they target oncology and autoimmune indications.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, SANA currently trades at a $1.2 billion market cap. Its latest 10-Q report from Q3 2025 shows its balance sheet held $103.4 million in cash and $49.7 million in marketable securities. That amounts to $153.1 million in liquid resources against no financial debt, though they do have regular operating liabilities. In total, their book value reached $195.3 million, indicating a high P/B of around 6.1. For comparison, their sector’s median P/B is closer to 2.9, so SANA would seem a bit expensive from that angle at these levels.

Moreover, I calculate SANA burned through roughly $29.4 million during Q3 2025. Note that I got this figure by simply adding its quarterly cash flows from operations and CAPEX. And this figure would imply a cash runway of around 5.2 quarters, which is a bit tight in my opinion. In fact, SANA itself admits it’s capital-constrained. Currently, most of their funds are going to T1D, and they cannot simultaneously run a fast in vivo CAR T development to treat blood cancers and autoimmune diseases.

Nevertheless, SANA listed targets like CD19 and BCMA and mentioned that a potential new partner would help them move into other indications and do it faster. SANA expects to have an early human proof-of-concept to secure a partnership on better terms (I assume in the near term). And, if we see a concrete announcement on this front, I would imagine that the stock would likely spike. After all, this would probably ease their financial constraints, while also helping progress with their pipeline more quickly. Plus, T1D itself is a huge TAM projected to reach $79.1 billion by 2035.

Ultimately, the bull case rests on SANA executing on its plans for SC451. Management aims for its IND and Phase 1 this year, and whenever we get an announcement for that milestone, it would be a great green flag for the stock by extension. And I do believe that SC451 is a unique asset, particularly because it promises a single treatment option that normalizes glucose for T1D. But after such an impressive run-up since the last time I covered them, I also have to temper my optimism due to their higher valuation and runway caveats. Plus, until we have tangible Phase 1 data, we can’t really know how safe this approach can be for humans, which makes SANA inherently speculative for now.

Conclusion: Bullish, But Take Some Profits

Overall, I think SANA’s stock appreciation since the last time I covered them warrants taking some profits at this point. After all, the stock is up almost 2x since then, but we still don’t have more concrete Phase 1 data for their lead asset (SC451). Similarly, I think it’s worth paying attention to SANA’s tight cash runway at this point. In theory, I believe they have enough funding to begin their Phase 1, and maybe even give us some early data from those trials to help them raise additional funds if they need them. So, at a $1.2 billion valuation without any late-stage candidates and a tight runway, the stock does seem a bit rich. Consequently, I do remain optimistic on the underlying science for T1D, but from an investment perspective, I feel it’s probably better to wait for dips before adding to your position.

data.title: Uncertain Times: Why Cash Flow Is The Ultimate Cure For Market Anxiety
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

My mother always said she wanted to come back in her next life as my dog. This is probably because my dogs tend to be slightly on the spoiled side. It's good to be a dog. Their biggest worries are whether or not dinner will be served on time and what those birds are doing on my lawn!

All my dogs are adopted, so they didn't have easy lives. One was brought back to the shelter at least three times in the span of a few months before we adopted her. Another had been living in the woods, running with the coyotes, before one fateful day, I'm typing away on my keyboard, and there is a dog staring in my office window. I like to think my mom sent her in my direction.

Sometimes dogs have rough lives, but one thing they don't have is worrying about tomorrow. Dogs are experts at "living in the moment." Provide them with food, care, and attention, and the scars of their past melt away. It doesn't matter how rough their life was before, or the risks they will face in the future. Today is a great day to soak up the sun, and that's the only thought going through their mind. Well, that and when is Dad going to get the peanut butter jar out?

Humans aren't good at living in the moment. We often struggle to get over what happened in the past. We worry about what might possibly happen in the future.

Since the beginning of time, people have looked for answers from oracles, fortune tellers, and prophets to predict the future. We obsess over the past, often turning to therapists, religious leaders, or elders to help us move on.

Ultimately, the stock market is about human nature. It is very much a reflection of us. That means investors obsess about predicting the future and struggle to move beyond the past.

Times Are Uncertain

Occasionally, people will say, "We live in uncertain times" - including me. It's a saying that is a bit silly when you think of it. Times are always uncertain. We never know for certain what is going to happen tomorrow.

Investors often turn to charts, with numerous strategies for recognizing patterns and trying to profit from them. These strategies work to the extent that the pattern repeats.

Others will try to predict news events and then predict how the market might respond. However, this means you have to accurately predict the news and accurately predict the market's response.

For example, suppose I told you that the unemployment rate was going to go up 100 bps. Would you be inclined to buy or sell stocks?

From 2023 through 2025, unemployment increased by 100 bps, and the S&P 500 rose in all three years.

If it doesn't make sense to you, that's because the stock market doesn't always make sense. It's a market that is fueled by human decisions, and human decisions aren't always logical.

As we sit here today, we see headlines about international conflicts, oil prices, interest rates, the labor market, domestic unrest, and changing laws. People often ask me "what happens if" and the answer is that any answer is at best an educated guess.

The only thing that is certain is that things are uncertain.

Living In The Past

Investors have a tendency to continue to do things that worked for them in the past and avoid things that didn't work. On its face, this makes sense, but in practice, it doesn't because conditions are always changing. If you read the disclaimers on every article, you'll see something along the lines of:

"Past performance is no guarantee of future results."

Indeed, past performance is very often the opposite of future results. The investments that worked best during a period of rising interest rates usually are not those that work best during a period of declining interest rates. An investment that went from a P/E ratio of 15x earnings to 25x earnings is going to have a very hard time having similar upside in the future. While valuations can get really high, it is also true that the higher a valuation gets, the less likely it is to go even higher.

The best time to buy an investment is when the price and valuations are low. Yet, when investors go to invest, they will look at a chart like this one:

It's a 10-year chart from 1990 to the end of 1999 for the S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY). Great returns, right? And they are eager to buy. Well, coming from the future, we know it was a horrible time to buy.

The following decade, investors in the S&P 500 made nothing - from 281% over 10 years to -9.64% the next 10 years.

The 10-year chart currently looks like this:

No, we don't know what the future will bring, but the past definitely informs us of what is possible.

Buying low means buying in 2009 when prices are down, and the 10-year chart is terrible. Selling high means selling in 2000, when the past 10 years have produced well above average returns. Most investors do the opposite.

How To Live In The Moment

There is always ample uncertainty, so our portfolios should always be prepared for a sustained downturn in the markets. We know they happen, and we know they happen shortly after the market is at its most optimistic point. The problem is, we don't know exactly where the "most optimistic point" is.

Every investor should have a plan for what they would do if their portfolio declined 50% and it took 10+ years to recover. Is that going to happen? We don't know. We do know it has happened in the past, and it will certainly happen eventually. If you are young and frequently adding new capital, it isn't such a big deal. You can keep adding capital, and as long as you don't panic and sell, you have time for your portfolio to recover. If you are nearing retirement or already retired, you need to have a plan so that you don't gut your retirement by selling at low prices.

With the Income Method, our strategy at High Dividend Opportunities is to generate a high level of income from interest and dividends. We have a healthy allocation to fixed-income holdings that pay a predictable amount of dividends regardless of share prices.

Build A Maturity Ladder

We maintain a maturity ladder with term preferred and baby bonds, structured in a way that we always have 4-8 maturities every single year. This way, maturities are happening numerous times a year and providing us with cash regardless of whether the market is up or down.

Our next maturity is OFS Credit Company, Inc. 6.125% Series C Term Preferred Stock Due 4/30/2026 (OCCIO). OCCI is a CEF (Closed-End Fund) that focuses on CLO equity investments. While CLO equity funds are very volatile, the CEF structure has numerous restrictions that make the preferred/bond levels of the capital structure much more stable. Their leverage is 0.76x, including preferred stock, which accounts for all of their "debt".

On April 30th, the company will pay us $25 par, and we will have the option to reinvest in other term-preferred issued by OFS Credit Company, in term-preferred or bonds from a different company, in a completely different investment, or we can just keep the cash. That's the power of cash flow; you don't have to predict the future. When you get the cash, you can decide in that moment the highest and best use for it.

Make Sure Cash Flow Exceeds Withdrawals

Share prices are volatile, and investors should assume that they are going to swing. It isn't unusual, it isn't rare, and it isn't a problem if you can simply hold. The problem comes in when you are forced to sell shares at a valuation that might be low.

Dividends aren't as volatile. Yes, dividends are sometimes cut, and in some cases, they are even eliminated forever. Not every investment is going to work out as planned. Overall, dividends are going to be much more stable than prices, and changes in dividends for better or worse usually reflect an actual change in the company's earnings.

Consider one of my favorite core funds, PIMCO Corporate and Income Opportunity Fund (PTY), yielding 10.9%. It's a fund that invests in a wide array of debt investments and is managed by PIMCO. PTY invests in everything from residential mortgages to high-yield corporate debt to non-US sovereign debt - providing a combination of credit risk and interest rate exposure.

Since 2003, PTY's dividend has varied, but with the exception of a particularly good year in 2013, PTY has paid a range of 9% to 15% yield on capital.

During the same period, the share price traded from a low of $5 and change to a high of over $22.

An investor selling PTY shares during the GFC was getting a poor price, as their $10,000 investment was selling for $5,000. An investor who just collected the $900 in dividends could just hold the shares and wait for the price to recover - which it did.

Our goal is to build a portfolio where the cash flow is enough to exceed our withdrawals and have a cushion large enough to allow for reinvesting 25%. This will help our cash flow grow and ensure we have a cushion to help absorb dividend cuts.

Maintain A Long-term Mindset

It's easy for investors to focus on what is happening right now and to label investments that are down in price as "bad" and those that are up in price as "good". This type of thinking is counterproductive when trying to build a portfolio that can survive and thrive through varying economic conditions.

An investor who was buying all the "good" investments in 1999 was loading up on tech, an investor who was buying all the "good" investments in 2007 was loading up on real estate, and it didn't end well for either of them.

Instead of focusing on buying everything that is doing good right now, a portfolio should be built contemplating the inevitability that things will change. A diversified portfolio should have exposure to a wide variety of industries. Yes, you want exposure to sectors that are hot, but you also want to be buying up sectors that will be hot in the future.

Conclusion

When you have a diversified portfolio that you can count on to produce substantial cash flow in all environments, then you can enjoy the luxury of living in the moment. You don't have to worry about what tomorrow will bring, because you know that you will have the cash flow to manage whatever challenges arise.

The great thing about cash flow is that it is always useful, and it can be redirected to suit your current needs. Having a portfolio where you are receiving significant amounts of interest and dividends every single month, goes a long way toward easing your mind of the uncertainties of the future and offsetting whatever mistakes of the past. That is how we invest at High Dividend Opportunities, and that is the power of income investing.

data.title: Fixed Income Perspectives: Resilient Markets, Selective Opportunities
data.description:
Introduction

Entering 2026, the fixed income landscape is increasingly being defined by a combination of sustained economic growth and the Federal Reserve’s anticipated slow drip rate cuts. With U.S. inflation remaining elevated, the Fed faces the challenging task of balancing persistent price pressures against a cooling labor market, but with healthy growth in the background. This situation is likely to result in fixed income returns aligning more closely with long-term averages, particularly in higher-quality segments such as investment-grade and securitized debt, which are known for their sensitivity to interest rate movements.

Policy volatility: Navigating geopolitical and economic challenges

The current environment is marked by policy volatility, as markets grapple with ongoing inflationary pressures and a decelerating U.S. labor market. The Fed’s easing cycle, occurring against a backdrop of sustained inflation exacerbated by geopolitical tensions (with Venezuela now added to the list) and the ever-present threat of tariff-based instability from the Trump Administration, creates a background of uncertainty. This scenario complicates investor sentiment and contributes to a steepening yield curve.

Credit fundamentals: Resilience amidst market dynamics

From a credit perspective, resilience is key. Investors should maintain a focus on robust technicals and credit fundamentals while remaining attentive to the overall economic conditions. The burst of growth in the third quarter of 2025 should help mitigate concerns about a recession, and that stubborn inflation can still be managed. Nevertheless, geopolitical headline risks and sensitivities to trade can lead to significant sector dispersion, underscoring the importance of active issuer selection and credit discipline.

Valuations: Further compression may be limited, but opportunities exist

Valuations present a complex picture: while further spread compression may be constrained, opportunities persist, especially within emerging markets, municipal bonds, securitized assets, and private credit. As dispersion replaces broad beta as the primary driver of returns, success in 2026 will hinge less on market direction and more on security selection. In this environment, disciplined active management will be essential to identify durable income, manage downside risks, and capture pockets of value amid ongoing volatility.

Perspectives from the CIO

MICHAEL GOOSAY

Chief Investment Officer, Fixed Income

The trajectory of interest rates will be pivotal in shaping fixed income outcomes in the year ahead. The Federal Reserve is expected to gradually lower policy rates toward a neutral level—likely around 3%—as it balances persistent inflation pressures against a labor market that is cooling but not collapsing. Against this backdrop, the opportunity set remains constructive, with fixed income offering attractive prospects for both income generation and total returns.

The prevailing economic conditions have laid a solid foundation for fixed income. With inflation projected to remain above the Fed’s 2% target, market expectations reflect a more accommodative approach to policy changes. This environment allows active investors to navigate sector dispersion and capitalize on favorable interest rates while remaining cautious.

The yield curve and duration positioning

Market participants should prepare for a potentially steeper yield curve as the Fed continues rate cuts. Historical patterns indicate that easing cycles typically lead to appreciation across the yield curve, providing prospects for capital gains alongside income generation. Investors are advised to maintain a moderately long duration stance to capitalize on shifts in the yield curve and evolving inflation expectations. Close attention to both ends of the curve will be essential for optimizing returns in this environment.

The intricate interplay of factors influencing the fixed income landscape cannot be overstated. Investors must remain vigilant regarding inflation trends and the Federal Reserve’s policy shifts, as these variables will significantly shape the yield curve and sector performance. Overall, the environment presents more favorable income rates compared to years past, offering the potential for reasonable total returns in 2026.

A favorable outlook, but agility is key

Strong technical conditions, robust fundamentals, and a supportive macroeconomic environment continue to underpin the fixed income market. However, the current landscape is complicated by tighter spreads. While demand for corporate debt and high-quality securities is expected to remain steady, new supply may surpass demand as companies capitalize on the Fed’s easing cycle to increase issuance. Additionally, consumer-focused credits may encounter challenges if the labor market shows signs of weakness. Overall, however, the market’s resiliency provides active managers with opportunities for effective bottom-up credit selection and sector differentiation.

Summary of investment implications

INVESTMENT GRADE CREDIT

Investment Grade (IG) credit should continue in good stead as an asset class, supported by strong carry, stable fundamentals, and manageable supply. All-in yields are historically attractive, and investor demand remains firm amid rate stability. We favor the 5- to 10-year part of the curve and see relative value in large U.S. banks and certain BBB- rated tech issuers linked to AI investment. While supply is expected to rise, technicals start from a strong position, underpinned by prevailing yields and investor demand. We anticipate that high-grade companies will capitalize on this investor receptivity. Monitoring technicals will be key as spreads remain near historical tights.

HIGH YIELD CREDIT

We remain constructive on high yield as a source of income, but upside is constrained by tight valuations and rising supply. Fundamentals are stable, and defaults are declining; yet modest spread widening is likely as issuance accelerates. Sector rotation—particularly growth in tech-linked issuers—is reshaping the market, creating new risks and opportunities. In this environment, we favor disciplined credit selection over beta exposure, emphasizing improving credit stories, rising stars, and sectors with durable cash flows.

SECURITIZED DEBT

Structured credit remains supported by strong demand, but deeper credit bifurcation is driving increased dispersion across sectors. We favor targeted exposure in agency MBS, especially call-protected conventional MBS, and see opportunities in discounted Ginnie Mae securities and SASB CMBS deals. Consumer ABS requires issuer-level selectivity amid softening fundamentals. Duration extension is tactical, with curve positioning more constrained than in other sectors. Active selection remains essential as fundamentals diverge and convexity risk rises.

MUNICIPALS

Munis offer attractive taxable-equivalent yields and a steep curve that rewards duration extension—especially versus Treasurys. With federal tax exemption concerns resolved, demand is expected to strengthen even amid elevated supply. We favor revenue-backed bonds tied to infrastructure, housing, and energy projects, while remaining cautious on general obligation bonds in fiscally pressured states. Active selection is becoming increasingly critical as credit dispersion increases and federal aid declines.

EMERGING MARKET DEBT

Emerging Market Debt (EMD) offers attractive carry and diversification, as fundamentals are sturdy and inflow momentum continues. Higher yields and lower correlations to developed market risks support valuations, especially amid fiscal pressure and AI-driven credit expansion in developed markets. We favor maintaining exposure across sovereigns and corporates while being prepared to take advantage of supply-driven volatility. Emerging economies and local currency debt may benefit from Fed easing and dollar weakness, while selective distressed names offer alpha potential for active managers.

PRIVATE CREDIT

Middle market direct lending enters 2026 with renewed momentum, supported by macro clarity, easing rates, and recovering valuations. Deal flow is reaccelerating, fund structures are evolving to meet investor needs, and fundamentals remain resilient, marked by low non-accruals and stable coverage ratios. We see a favorable setup for new deployment, with strong risk-adjusted return potential and less volatility than public markets. Structural discipline and credit selection will be key to navigating the next phase of market maturity.

Macro

U.S. outlook

2025 was not without its challenges: sticky inflation, a cooling labor market, elevated supply across sectors, volatile trade policy, and ongoing geopolitical uncertainty. Yet despite these headwinds, fixed income markets delivered resilient performance—driven by a combination of disinflation, late-year Fed rate cuts, a resilient consumer, and the early effects of AI-driven capital spending. The result: the highest total return for the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index in the post-COVID era.

Looking ahead to 2026, we remain optimistic about the outlook for fixed income, supported by a mix of cyclical and structural tailwinds:

  • Continued labor market softening should keep the Fed on track for further rate cuts.
  • Rising productivity—partly tied to early AI adoption—may help sustain economic growth even as employment moderates.
  • Inflation is expected to peak in the first half of 2026 and trend toward the Fed’s 2% target by year-end.
  • AI-related capex is projected to accelerate meaningfully relative to 2025.
  • Consumer spending remains steady with limited signs of fatigue.
  • Corporate fundamentals, including margins and balance sheets, remain robust.
  • U.S. fiscal policy is pivoting from tariff and trade uncertainty toward pro-growth tax cuts and deregulation, delivering a strong fiscal impulse early in the year.
  • Yields remain attractive by historical standards, offering favorable entry points for long-term investors.

Market environment

Year-to-date performance, spread, and yield for various fixed income indices

1 Total returns for representative indices. 2 Spread to Treasury. Min, max, and average based on last 10 years. 3 Index yield to worst. Min, max, and average based on last 10 years. Weighted average yield-to-maturity reflected for U.S. Bank Loans. Indices are unmanaged and do not take into account fees, expenses, and transaction costs, and it is not possible to invest in an index.

Source: Bloomberg, Principal Asset Management. Data are as of December 31, 2025.

That said, several risks warrant monitoring. Tight credit spreads and elevated valuations limit room for error. Market expectations for rapid productivity gains may overshoot the near-term return on AI investment. Increased issuance tied to infrastructure and technology capex could challenge absorption, particularly if Treasury term premia continue to rise. Meanwhile, a deeper labor market slowdown could raise recession fears, and any perceived erosion of Fed independence could add volatility to the policy outlook.

We maintain a constructive view for 2026. Volatility is likely to present buying opportunities, not reasons to retreat. We believe fixed income remains well-positioned to potentially deliver solid, income-driven returns in the year ahead.

Global outlook

As 2026 begins, the global macro environment is shaped by defining forces that present a compelling mix of challenges and opportunities: a widening divergence in monetary policy across developed economies, a synchronized shift toward fiscal expansion, and evolving geopolitical dynamics. Together, these dynamics have significant implications for growth forecasts, capital flows, and relative value across global fixed income markets.

Diverging central bank paths signal an end to the synchronous easing cycle. While the Federal Reserve is expected to extend its easing cycle amid rising unemployment and political pressure, other major central banks are either nearing the end of their easing cycles—or actively preparing to hike. The ECB, Bank of Canada, and Reserve Bank of New Zealand have already signaled a shift away from further cuts, while the Reserve Bank of Australia and Riksbank (Sweden) are openly considering tightening scenarios in 2026.

Japan stands out as the most hawkish. The Bank of Japan brought forward rate hikes in late 2025 and advanced the timeline for further tightening—marking a historic pivot from ultra-accommodative policy, which had been cemented in place for generations. With policy rates likely to rise further in 2026, Japan’s role as a global capital exporter may begin to shift, potentially leading to ripple effects across global duration markets.

Fiscal policy turns more expansionary

Fiscal expansion is re-emerging as a global theme, supported by political incentives and geopolitical realignment. Europe is experiencing renewed momentum in defense and infrastructure spending, despite previous concerns about fiscal discipline. Japan’s new leadership is embracing an “Abenomics 2.0” approach—pursuing growth through aggressive fiscal policy and expanded public investment. Even the U.S., once expected to moderate its deficits, has turned the fiscal dial higher, and midterm election incentives are likely to drive proposals for household tax rebates and revived corporate tax breaks.

The investment implications are nuanced. We expect global yields to remain elevated in aggregate, although U.S. Treasury yields may ease somewhat in response to Fed cuts. That suggests limited scope for further capital appreciation in rates markets, and a renewed focus on carry and credit selection. At the same time, diverging rate paths are likely to support a weaker U.S. dollar, albeit with smaller depreciation than seen in 2025. This could continue to drive interest in local currency EM debt and other non-dollar assets.

Geopolitics remains in transition

Europe remains the focal point, while the ongoing conflict between Russia and Ukraine remains a concern, there is lingering hope for a potential ceasefire. Meanwhile, U.S.-China tensions have entered a temporary détente, reducing near-term trade risk as both governments prioritize domestic agendas ahead of the U.S. midterm elections. However, China faces its own challenges, including weak household spending and a decline in infrastructure investment, which may require targeted policy support moving forward. Lastly, the markets also face reduced concerns regarding trade wars compared to a year ago; however, investors will continue to monitor the upcoming U.S. Supreme Court decision on tariffs and the Administration’s reaction.

Outlook: cautiously optimistic, with relative upside risk

While structural headwinds remain, particularly in Europe and China, there is scope for relative improvement compared to subdued guidance from official institutions like the IMF. For fixed income investors, the focus should remain on active allocation across regions, disciplined duration exposure, and a realistic outlook for returns—driven more by income than price gains in 2026.

Investment grade credit

Strong carry, manageable supply, and curve steepening support a favorable start to 2026

The outlook for investment grade credit remains constructive heading into 2026. While gross supply is expected to increase this year, driven in part by capital investment in AI infrastructure, the asset class continues to benefit from a stable economic environment, positive investor flows, and an attractive yield profile that serves as a stabilizing factor. Against this backdrop, IG credit remains an appealing asset class for investors seeking income with low default risk and relatively low duration.

Yield remains the anchor

Even with spreads near the tighter end of historical ranges, all-in yields for IG corporates remain in the 70th percentile historically. That’s been a key draw for investors, reflected in a 32-week streak of inflows—the second-longest streak ever—that lasted until mid-December 2025. The combination of resilient carry and low realized volatility continues to reinforce IG’s appeal versus other risk assets.

Rate stability has also played a role. While further policy easing is expected in 2026, the 10-year Treasury has traded within a tight range, and only a modest decline is expected from here. That stability supports roll-down strategies and relative value across the 5- to 10-year belly of the curve, which is a favored positioning segment.

Supply to rise—but remains digestible

The IG market is preparing for a manageable uptick in primary issuance in 2026. Gross supply could approach $1.8 trillion this year, with net supply forecasts around $300 billion. The expected increase in both gross and net supply is certainly tenable in the context of strong demand, elevated maturities, and a growing appetite from both domestic and international investors.

A key theme for 2026 is the anticipated rise in long-duration debt issuance, with technology companies leading the way by investing in generative AI infrastructure. High-quality “hyperscaler” names, such as Amazon, Meta, and Oracle, are expected to be active issuers, helping reshape the top tier of IG supply. These firms are expected to use IG debt to finance their investments in AI, with projections suggesting $200 to $300 billion in new issuance over the next five years. At the same time, bank issuance is likely to decline, partly due to regulatory changes such as SLR (supplementary leverage ratio) reform, which reduces supply pressures from the financial sector.

Sector themes and positioning

Financials remain a compelling sector. Fundamentals across large, diversified banks are robust, characterized by solid asset quality, stable deposits, and healthy capital levels. Banks are also well-positioned to capitalize on growing M&A activity. Ongoing deregulation could further support margins by reducing operating costs, making the sector increasingly resemble the “new utilities” of credit. Select Yankee banks (non-U.S. domiciled banks that issue in U.S. dollars) also offer relative value, particularly where regulatory clarity supports fundamental stability.

We also maintain a constructive view on AI-exposed technology issuers, particularly among BBB-rated names that play niche roles in the AI ecosystem.
